title: The library card my father kept
summary: After their father dies, two siblings find a 1973 library card in his drawer. He never read books — so why did he keep it for fifty years?
body:
My father kept his old things in one drawer. Watches, broken pens, a small ring of keys for doors that did not exist anymore. After he died, my brother and I opened the drawer to clean it.

We found a small paper card. The corners were soft. The writing was almost gone. But we could read three things: my father's name, an address, and the year 1973.

It was a library card.

I was surprised. My father did not read books. He read the newspaper at breakfast and watched the news at night. I had never seen him with a book.

I asked my mother. She thought for a moment. Then she smiled.

'When your father was eighteen,' she said, 'he was in love with a girl who worked at the library. He went there every Saturday. He sat at a desk. He pretended to read so he could look at her.'

'Did he ever speak to her?' I asked.

'No. He was very shy. After two years, the girl moved away.'

'Then why did he keep the card for fifty years?'

My mother shook her head. 'I do not know. But he did.'

I put the card back in the drawer. We did not throw it away.

grammarNotes.title: Past perfect to mark an earlier past
grammarNotes.bodyVi: Dùng thì quá khứ hoàn thành (had + V3) để chỉ một việc đã xảy ra TRƯỚC một thời điểm khác trong quá khứ. Trong bài: 'I had never seen him with a book.' (Tôi chưa từng thấy bố cầm một cuốn sách.) Hành động 'never seen' (chưa từng thấy) xảy ra trước khoảnh khắc tìm thấy thẻ thư viện.
grammarNotes.bodyEn: Use the past perfect (had + past participle) for an action that happened BEFORE another past event. From the story: 'I had never seen him with a book.' The 'never seeing' sits before the moment of finding the library card.

blanks.explanation: He kept his old things in one drawer.
Broken pens — among the things in the drawer.
He read the newspaper at breakfast — but not books.
When he was eighteen, he was in love with the girl.
He sat at a desk — pretending to read.
He pretended to read so he could look at her.
He was very shy — and never spoke to her.
He kept the card for fifty years.
She shook her head — she did not know why he kept it.

translationVi:
Bố tôi giữ những đồ vật cũ trong một ngăn kéo. Đồng hồ, bút bi hỏng, một chùm chìa khóa cho những cánh cửa không còn tồn tại nữa. Sau khi bố mất, anh tôi và tôi mở ngăn kéo để dọn.

Chúng tôi tìm thấy một tấm thẻ giấy nhỏ. Các góc đã mềm nhũn. Chữ gần như mờ hết. Nhưng chúng tôi đọc được ba điều: tên bố tôi, một địa chỉ, và năm 1973.

Đó là một tấm thẻ thư viện.

Tôi ngạc nhiên. Bố tôi không đọc sách. Bố đọc báo lúc ăn sáng và xem tin tức buổi tối. Tôi chưa từng thấy bố cầm một cuốn sách.

Tôi hỏi mẹ. Mẹ suy nghĩ một lát. Rồi mẹ mỉm cười.

'Khi bố con mười tám tuổi,' mẹ nói, 'bố con đem lòng yêu một cô gái làm việc ở thư viện. Bố con đến đó mỗi thứ Bảy. Bố con ngồi ở một cái bàn. Bố con giả vờ đọc sách để có thể nhìn cô ấy.'

'Bố con có nói chuyện với cô ấy không ạ?' tôi hỏi.

'Không. Bố con rất nhút nhát. Sau hai năm, cô gái đó chuyển đi nơi khác.'

'Vậy sao bố con giữ tấm thẻ năm mươi năm ạ?'

Mẹ lắc đầu. 'Mẹ không biết. Nhưng bố con đã giữ.'

Tôi đặt tấm thẻ trở lại trong ngăn kéo. Chúng tôi đã không vứt nó đi.

questions.prompt: What did the father keep in his drawer?
options: Old things: watches, broken pens, keys for doors that did not exist anymore

questions.prompt: What three things were still readable on the small card?
options: The father's age, address, and phone number
The father's name, an address, and the year 1973
A signature, a date, and a stamp
questions.explanation: 'My father's name, an address, and the year 1973.'

questions.prompt: What kind of card was it?

options: His father did not read books — he had never seen him with a book
questions.explanation: 'My father did not read books… I had never seen him with a book.'

questions.prompt: Why did the father go to the library when he was eighteen?
options: He was in love with a girl who worked there
questions.explanation: 'He was in love with a girl who worked at the library.'

questions.prompt: What did he do at the library?
options: He pretended to read so he could look at her
He spoke to her every week
questions.explanation: 'He pretended to read so he could look at her.'

questions.prompt: Did he ever speak to the girl?
options: No — he was very shy, and after two years the girl moved away
questions.explanation: 'No. He was very shy. After two years, the girl moved away.'

questions.prompt: Does the mother know why he kept the card for fifty years?
options: Yes — because he might go back
No — but she confirms that he did keep it
She refuses to talk about it
questions.explanation: 'I do not know. But he did.'
questions.hint: Look at the mother's last words.

questions.prompt: What does the family do with the card at the end?
options: Put it back in the drawer; they do not throw it away
questions.explanation: 'I put the card back in the drawer. We did not throw it away.'

criticalThinkingQuestion: The writer's father kept a small paper card for fifty years, even though he 'did not read books'. Think of an old object you have that does not seem useful — but you still keep. What does its smallness keep visible that nothing else does?
